walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Above and Below: Haunted set up. Everybody grab a player board. You'll notice it has three main sections: a large area on the left called the ready area, a middle section with a moon symbol called the exhausted area, and a small section on the right called the injured area. There's also a row of circles along the bottom, which is your advancement track. We'll get to all of these in a minute.

walkthrough.title: Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player grabs 8 coins and 1 mystic brew token from the supply. Mystic brews are the green potion-looking tokens. Keep your coins in a personal pile near your board. You'll also grab 1 starting house card and place it to the right of your player board. This is the foundation of your village.

walkthrough.title: Collect Starting Villagers
walkthrough.text: Now find the starting villagers. These are the ones with a house icon on the back. Each player takes 3 starting villagers: one with a hammer symbol, one with a quill symbol, and one without either. Place all three in the ready area on your player board. Quick heads up for four-player games: only your hammer villager starts in the ready area. Your other two go in the exhausted area and won't be available until the end of round one.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Star and Key Houses
walkthrough.text: Lay out all 8 star house cards face up in a row where everyone can see them. These premium buildings are available to buy throughout the entire game. Next, shuffle the 9 key house cards, draw 5 at random, and lay them face up in a row below the star houses. Put the remaining key house cards back in the box. Key houses have powerful effects, so take a look at what's available.

walkthrough.title: Place the Reputation Board
walkthrough.text: Put the reputation board in the center of the table. This is command central. It has the reputation track running down the side, the time track for counting rounds, the ingredient track for claiming goods, and spaces along the top for the villager market. Set the 2 special villagers aside for now. Shuffle the remaining regular villagers face down into a pile, then draw 5 and place them along the top of the reputation board. These are the villagers available for hire.

walkthrough.title: Round Marker and Reputation Cubes
walkthrough.text: Place the round marker on the first slot of the time track on the reputation board. Then each player grabs a set of wooden cubes matching their player board's banner color. Everyone places one cube on the torch space of the reputation track. This is your starting reputation position. Keep your remaining cubes handy for the ingredient track.

walkthrough.title: Stock the Supply
walkthrough.text: Create a central supply area with all the tokens within everyone's reach. This includes the coin tokens, all the goods tokens, mystic brew tokens, ghost tokens, pearl tokens, and boat tokens. You'll be dipping into this supply throughout the game, so spread it out so nothing gets buried.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Cave Cards
walkthrough.text: Separate the cave cards into two piles. Basic caves have a simple design, while dangerous caves have a red serpent in the water and a pearl icon in the top left corner. Shuffle each pile separately and place them face up next to the reputation board. You'll draw from these when you explore.

walkthrough.title: House and Outpost Card Rows
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the house cards and outpost cards into two separate face-down decks. Place them near the reputation board. Draw the top 4 cards from each deck and lay them face up in a row next to their deck. These are the buildings available for purchase. When someone buys one, you'll draw a replacement from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Adventure Cards and Encounter Book
walkthrough.text: Place the stack of adventure cards face down near the play area. Important: keep them in numerical order and don't peek at the faces until they're earned during encounters. Place the encounter book and all 7 dice where everyone can reach them. The encounter book is the heart of this game. When you explore underground, someone will read you a story from it.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player and Bonus Coins
walkthrough.text: Decide who goes first and give them the first-player card. Turn order goes clockwise. Now hand out bonus coins based on player count to keep things fair. In a two-player game, the second player gets 1 extra coin. In a three-player game, the second and third players each get 1 extra coin. In a four-player game, the second and third players get 1 extra coin each, and the fourth player gets 2 extra coins.

walkthrough.title: Setup Checkpoint
walkthrough.text: Let's make sure everyone is ready. Each player should have a player board with 3 villagers in the ready area, a starting house card beside the board, 8 coins plus any bonus coins, and 1 mystic brew. In the center you should see the reputation board with 5 villagers for hire, the star houses and key houses laid out, two rows of 4 buildings each for houses and outposts, two face-up cave card piles, adventure cards, the encounter book, and dice. If anything is missing, now's the time to fix it. You're ready to play!

summary.title: Setup Checkpoint
bullets: Each player: board, 3 villagers ready, starting house, 8+ coins, 1 mystic brew
Center: reputation board with 5 villagers, star/key houses, 2x4 building rows, 2 cave piles, adventure cards, encounter book, dice

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Here's how the game flows. You'll play exactly 7 rounds. Each round, starting with the first player and going clockwise, everyone takes turns doing one action at a time. When you're out of ready villagers or just don't want to do anything else, you say 'I pass.' Once everyone has passed, the round ends and you do some cleanup. Then the next round starts.

walkthrough.title: Choosing Your Action
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you'll pick one of five actions: Explore, Build, Banish, Hire, or Labor. Each one requires you to slide one or more villagers from your ready area to the exhausted area or beyond. Once a villager is exhausted, they're done for the round. You can also do any number of free actions before or after your main action. We'll cover those in a bit.

walkthrough.title: Explore: Drawing a Cave Card
walkthrough.text: Here's what makes this game unlike anything else on the shelf. When you explore, you send your villagers underground into mysterious caves. First, draw a cave card from the top of either the basic or dangerous pile and place it beside your player board. Then slide 2 or more villagers from your ready area onto that cave card. You need at least 2 villagers to explore, but you can send more for better odds.

walkthrough.title: Explore: The Encounter
walkthrough.text: Now for the fun part. Roll one die and check the grid on the cave card. That grid tells you which paragraph number to look up in the encounter book. The player to your left opens the book, finds that paragraph, and reads the whole thing aloud. They'll read the story description and the choices in bold, along with the explore numbers. But they don't read the rewards in parentheses. That part stays secret until you succeed.

walkthrough.title: Explore: Rolling and Counting Lanterns
walkthrough.text: After hearing the encounter, you pick one of the choices and announce it. Now you need to meet or beat its explore number. Roll 1 die for each villager you sent, one at a time, placing each die on its villager. Here's how lanterns work: a roll of 4, 5, or 6 gives you 2 lanterns. A roll of 2 or 3 gives you 1 lantern. And a roll of 1 gives you nothing. Add up all your lanterns and compare to the explore number.

walkthrough.title: Explore: Success, Failure, and Exerting
walkthrough.text: If your lantern total meets or exceeds the explore number, you succeed! The reader turns to the reward paragraph and reads your spoils. If you exceed the number by 2 or more, you also get a bonus reward. If you fail, you don't get rewards, but you still keep the cave card. Here's a key tool: after rolling, you can exert any villager by moving them to the injured area instead of exhausted. Each exerted villager gives you plus 1 extra lantern. It's risky because injured villagers are harder to recover, but sometimes that extra push is worth it.

walkthrough.title: Explore: Dangerous Caves and Pearls
walkthrough.text: You'll notice there are two types of cave cards. Basic caves have explore numbers between 2 and 6. Dangerous caves, marked with the red serpent, have explore numbers between 5 and 11. They're tougher, but if you succeed at a dangerous cave, you also earn a pearl token. Pearls are incredibly valuable because at the end of the game, each pearl counts as your highest-valued good. Send your best villagers and consider using a boat token to guarantee a 6 on one die.

walkthrough.title: Build Action
walkthrough.text: To build, slide one villager with a hammer symbol from your ready area to exhausted. Then choose any available building: a house card, star house, key house, or outpost. Pay the coin cost shown on the card. Houses, key houses, and star houses go in a row next to your starting house on the surface. Outposts are different. They go underground, placed on top of an empty cave card you already own. If you don't have an empty cave card, you can't build an outpost. When you buy from the house or outpost row, draw a replacement from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Banish Action
walkthrough.text: This is new to Haunted. If you have at least one ghost on your buildings, you can banish them. Slide one villager with a quill symbol to exhausted. Then return all ghosts from your buildings to the supply except one. Take that last ghost and place it on one of your opponent's buildings that doesn't already have a ghost. You get a reward based on what type of building you haunt: a building without a bed gets you 2 reputation and 2 coins, a house with a bed gets you 1 mystic brew and 3 coins, and an outpost gets you 1 mystic brew and 1 boat.

walkthrough.title: Hire Action
walkthrough.text: To hire a new villager, slide one quill villager to exhausted. Look at the 5 villagers displayed along the top of the reputation board. Each one has a coin cost listed below it. Pay the cost and place the new villager in your exhausted area. Here's the twist: the first 3 slots are cheaper because those villagers take the short route through the haunted marshland. If you hire from slots 1, 2, or 3, you also gain a ghost token and must place it on one of your buildings. If you hire from slot 4 or 5, you pay more but get a mystic brew instead of a ghost.

walkthrough.title: Labor Action
walkthrough.text: Labor is your money and goods action. Slide one or more villagers from ready to exhausted. For each villager you exhaust, you gain 1 coin and you may also harvest 1 good from one of your houses or outposts. If you use a villager with the hammer symbol, that villager can harvest 2 goods instead of 1, from different buildings if you like. Harvested goods can be kept for later, placed for sale, or added to your advancement track.

walkthrough.title: Free Actions
walkthrough.text: Before or after your main action, you can do as many free actions as you want without exhausting anyone. You can put a good or mystic brew up for sale. You can buy from another player's sale area for at least 3 coins or mystic brews. You can pay 1 coin to refresh all 4 cards in either the house or outpost row, but only once per turn. And you can claim a new ingredient, which we'll talk about next.

walkthrough.title: Boats and the Ingredient Track
walkthrough.text: Two more free actions. First, boats. When you have a boat token in your ready area, you can move it to exhausted to either gain 1 coin or, during an explore action, change any one die to a 6. You can't do both with the same boat. Second, the ingredient track. The first time anyone in the game harvests or gains a particular type of good, that player places one of their cubes on that good's space on the ingredient track. This is worth points at the end: 1 point for common goods like rice, clams, and crabs, 2 points for uncommon goods, and 4 points for rare goods like squidroot and rubyfish.

walkthrough.title: The Advancement Track
walkthrough.text: Look at the bottom of your player board. That row of circles is your advancement track. Whenever you have goods, you can place them here from left to right with no gaps. If you get another good of the same type, it stacks on the same slot. The number above the left side of each slot tells you how many village points each good there is worth at game end. The number on the right side shows your current income level. So the more goods types you place, the more coins you collect each round and the more points you score.

walkthrough.title: Beds and Refreshing Villagers
walkthrough.text: Here's the core puzzle of the game. After your villagers work, they get exhausted, and they won't recover until the end of the round. Many buildings have bed symbols on them. At the end of each round, for each bed you own across all your buildings, you can move one villager one step: either from injured to exhausted, or from exhausted to ready. You can also spend mystic brew tokens the same way, one token per villager. Beds are essential. Without them, your villagers will pile up in the exhausted area and you'll run out of workers fast.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Sequence
walkthrough.text: When everyone has passed, follow these steps in order. First, ghosts haunt: lose 1 reputation for each ghost on your buildings. Second, advance the round marker. Third, slide any remaining villagers on the reputation board to the left to fill the cheapest slots, then draw new villagers to fill any empty spaces. Fourth, rest your villagers using beds and mystic brews. Boats also return to your ready area automatically. Fifth, collect income based on your advancement track. Sixth, refresh any goods on buildings that have the refill symbol. Seventh, pass the first-player card to the left. Then start the next round.

walkthrough.title: Ghosts and Reputation
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about ghosts a bit more because they're a big part of this game. Ghosts show up mainly when you hire cheap villagers through the haunted marsh. Each ghost sits on one of your buildings, and at the end of every round, each ghost costs you 1 reputation. That adds up quickly. You lose points if your reputation drops too low, and you gain bonus points for having the highest reputation at the end. Use the banish action to clear them out and send one to your opponent's village.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Scoring
walkthrough.text: After 7 rounds, it's time to count up village points. You score in several categories. First, each good on your advancement track scores based on its slot position. Second, each building you own is worth 1 village point, including your starting house. Third, the player with the highest reputation gets 5 bonus points, second gets 3, third gets 2. You also gain or lose points based on your exact position on the reputation track. Fourth, check your building cards for any bonus points printed on them. Fifth, score your ingredient track cubes. And finally, each pearl counts as your highest-valued good. Add it all up. Most village points wins! Ties go to most coins, then most villagers and boats, then most buildings.

Round ends when all players pass

walkthrough.title: Respect the 7-Round Clock
walkthrough.text: The single most important thing to understand about this game is that you only have 7 rounds. That sounds like a lot, but it goes fast. With 3 starting villagers, you might only get 3 actions per round early on. That's roughly 20 to 30 total actions in the entire game. Every single one needs to count. Don't waste rounds 1 and 2 figuring out your strategy. Have a plan from the start.

walkthrough.title: Hire Early and Often
walkthrough.text: More villagers means more actions per round. Getting a fourth or fifth villager in rounds 1 or 2 means you'll have that extra action for 5 or 6 remaining rounds. That's huge. Don't be afraid to hire from the cheap slots and pick up a ghost. One or two ghosts early is very manageable, and the cost savings let you invest elsewhere. If you wait until round 4 to start hiring, you've missed half the game.

walkthrough.title: Balance Exploring and Building
walkthrough.text: Exploring does three things: it gives you a cave card for future outpost construction, it gives you encounter rewards like goods, coins, and adventure cards, and it's genuinely fun. But building is how you get beds, income, and points. A strong pattern is to explore in rounds 1 and 2 to stockpile cave cards and rewards, then shift to building outposts and houses in rounds 3 through 5. Late game rounds 6 and 7 should be about scoring: labor for goods, build for that last key house, and make sure your advancement track is full.

walkthrough.title: Read Encounters Wisely
walkthrough.text: When you hear the encounter choices, think about your odds. If you sent 2 villagers, your expected lanterns are about 2 to 4 total. So an explore number of 3 is reasonable, but 5 or higher is a gamble. With 3 villagers, you can comfortably aim for explore 5 or 6. Remember you can exert villagers for plus 1 lantern each, and boat tokens can change a die to 6. Factor those options in before you pick the harder choice. Higher explore numbers generally give better rewards, so push when you can afford to.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Ghosts
walkthrough.text: A ghost or two is fine. Three or four ghosts bleeding reputation every round will wreck your score. Each ghost costs you 1 reputation per round, and that compounds over the whole game. The banish action is powerful because it clears all your ghosts at once and lets you dump one on an opponent. Time your banish for when you have 3 or more ghosts, not just 1. The rewards from banishing are solid too, especially the mystic brew and boat from haunting an outpost.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Advancement Track
walkthrough.text: Your starting income is only 3 coins. That's not enough to hire, build, and refresh comfortably. Getting different goods types onto your advancement track quickly bumps your income to 5, 6, or even 7 coins per round. Variety is better than quantity here. Two different goods in slots 1 and 2 are worth more income-wise than stacking 3 of the same good in slot 1. Also try to be the first player to harvest each goods type so you can claim the ingredient track spot. Those rare good spots are worth 4 points each.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Beds
walkthrough.text: Beds are the engine oil of this game. Without beds, your exhausted villagers just sit there until the next round starts, and even then they only recover if you have enough beds. Buy buildings with bed symbols early. Each bed essentially gives you one more ready villager per round. A building with 2 beds is worth far more than its coin cost suggests because it keeps your workforce active. Mystic brews help too, but beds are permanent and brews are one-time use.

walkthrough.title: Putting It All Together
walkthrough.text: Here's a solid game plan for your first play. Rounds 1 and 2: explore to get cave cards and encounter rewards, hire one cheap villager even if it means a ghost. Round 3: build an outpost on a cave card and maybe a house with beds. Round 4: banish your ghosts if you have them, keep exploring or building. Rounds 5 and 6: labor to harvest goods, fill your advancement track, build remaining outposts. Round 7: squeeze out every last point. Remember, every building is worth 1 village point on its own, so even a cheap house matters. Good luck down there in the caves.

summary.title: Respect the 7-Round Clock
bullets: Only 7 rounds with ~20-30 total actions
Have a plan from round 1; no time for slow starts

summary.title: Hire Early and Often
bullets: More villagers = more actions per round for rest of game
Cheap hires (slots 1-3) give ghosts but save coins
Aim to have 4-5 villagers by round 3

summary.title: Balance Exploring and Building

Build rounds 3-5 (outposts on caves, houses for beds/income)
Score rounds 6-7 (labor, final builds, fill advancement track)

summary.title: Read Encounters Wisely

Exerting gives +1 lantern per villager; boats change a die to 6
Higher explore numbers generally give better rewards

summary.title: Diversify Your Advancement Track

entries.definition: Draw a cave card, send 2 or more villagers, roll dice, resolve an encounter from the encounter book. Gain rewards on success. You keep the cave card even on failure.
Exhaust 1 hammer villager to purchase a house, star house, key house, or outpost by paying its coin cost. Outposts require an empty cave card.
Exhaust 1 quill villager when you have ghosts. Return all ghosts except 1 to supply, place that 1 on an opponent's building. Gain a reward based on the haunted building type.
Exhaust 1 quill villager to pay for and recruit a villager from the reputation board. Slots 1-3 gain a ghost; slots 4-5 gain a mystic brew. New villager goes to exhausted area.
Exhaust 1 or more villagers. Gain 1 coin per villager and may harvest 1 good per villager from your buildings. Hammer villagers harvest 2 goods instead of 1.
Declare you are done for the round. You may take free actions before passing but cannot take any actions after.
Your personal board with three villager areas (ready, exhausted, injured) and an advancement track along the bottom for placing goods.
Central board with the reputation track, time track for rounds, villager market along the top, and the ingredient track for claiming first harvest of each good type.
Numbered paragraphs with branching narrative encounters. When exploring, the player to your left reads the encounter and choices aloud. They do not read the rewards in parentheses.
Cards representing underground locations. Basic caves have explore numbers 2-6. Dangerous caves (red serpent icon) have explore numbers 5-11 and award a pearl on success.
43 unique cards gained as encounter rewards. Each provides a special ability as described on the card. Kept in numerical order and not viewed until earned.
Surface buildings placed in a row next to your starting house. Provide beds, goods, income, and other effects.
Underground buildings placed on top of empty cave cards. Often score more than surface buildings but require prior exploration.
8 premium buildings always available for purchase. Not replaced when bought. Offer strong effects and VP.
5 powerful buildings selected randomly from 9 at setup. Always available for purchase. Often have game-changing ongoing effects.
Purchase a good or mystic brew marked for sale by another player. Must pay at least 3 coins or mystic brews. Seller may refuse.
Pay 1 coin to replace all 4 cards in either the house or outpost row. Once per turn. Cannot refresh star or key houses.
When you are the first player to gain a good type, place your cube on the ingredient track. Worth 1 VP (common), 2 VP (uncommon), or 4 VP (rare) at game end.
Common goods. Most frequently available. Worth 1 VP on the ingredient track. Easiest to harvest from buildings.
Uncommon goods. Moderately available. Worth 2 VP on the ingredient track.
Rare goods. Hard to find. Worth 4 VP on the ingredient track. Very valuable for advancement track scoring.
Tokens placed on your buildings, primarily gained from cheap hires (slots 1-3). Each ghost costs 1 reputation at end of round. Max 1 ghost per building. Cleared via banish action.
Gained from successful dangerous cave explorations. At game end, each pearl counts as your highest-valued good type in the advancement track.
Tokens in your ready area. Exhaust to gain 1 coin OR change any die to 6 during exploration. Cannot do both. Return to ready at end of round without needing beds.
Token used during end-of-round rest to move 1 villager one zone (injured to exhausted or exhausted to ready). Also used as currency when buying from players (counts toward the 3 minimum).
The skill check currency during exploration. Gained from dice rolls: 4-6 = 2 lanterns, 2-3 = 1 lantern, 1 = 0. Also +1 per exerted villager. Must meet or exceed explore number to succeed.
Symbols on buildings. Each bed moves 1 villager one zone at end of round: injured to exhausted, or exhausted to ready. Essential for keeping your workforce active.
After rolling during exploration, move a villager to injured (instead of exhausted) to gain +1 lantern. Injured villagers need mystic brew or extra beds to recover.
Row on player board bottom. Place goods left to right, same type stacks. Left number = VP per good at end. Right number = current income. Once placed, goods stay permanently.
Most reputation: 5 VP, second: 3 VP, third: 2 VP. Two-player: leader gets 3 VP only. Ties split summed VP rounded up. Also gain or lose VP from track position.
Each building owned (houses, star houses, key houses, outposts, starting house) is worth 1 VP. Empty cave cards do not count.
VP for cubes placed when claiming first harvest of a good type. Common (rice, clam, crab) = 1 VP. Uncommon (tea leaf, nut, thornfruit) = 2 VP. Rare (squidroot, rubyfish) = 4 VP.
Villager with a hammer symbol. Required for the Build action. Harvests 2 goods instead of 1 during Labor.
Villager with a quill symbol. Required for the Hire and Banish actions.
Special villagers gained from encounters. Each time assigned to an action, roll a die. Roll 1-2: cat refuses and goes to exhausted without doing anything. Roll 3-6: works normally.
Special villager that cannot use building beds or mystic brews to recover. Has its own built-in bed.
Left section of player board. Villagers here are available to assign to actions this round.
Middle section of player board (moon symbol). Villagers moved here after being assigned. Recover to ready via beds at end of round.
Right section of player board. Villagers exerted during exploration go here. Require mystic brew or beds to move to exhausted, then another bed to reach ready.
